audit_trail.notes: Reviewed artifact is a ScienceDaily institutional press release, not the primary Nature Physics article. All three reviewers converged by cycle 2 on the two major objections (WF law mischaracterization; false 'first time' novelty claim). Cycle 2 brought Gemini into alignment with Claude and Codex on novelty severity after Claude cited specific prior-art references. Codex noted in cycle 2 that the Nature Physics abstract independently corroborates the >200× WF violation and viscosity claims at abstract level, slightly raising confidence in the underlying result without making the press-release claims auditable. No reviewer had access to the full primary paper; all quantitative assessments are inferred from the press release and abstract-level knowledge of the field.

general_reader_takeaway: A ScienceDaily press release claims that graphene has "broken a fundamental law of physics" for the first time, but the review board found the headline substantially misleading on two counts: the Wiedemann-Franz law is a Fermi-liquid approximation that was never expected to hold at graphene's Dirac point, and experimental observation of this breakdown was already reported by Crossno et al. in Science in 2016. The underlying Nature Physics paper appears to be legitimate condensed-matter research, with the credible novel contribution being the demonstration of universal quantum-of-conductance scaling and a larger violation factor achieved in cleaner samples. The most important caveat is that all conclusions here are drawn from a promotional press release with no data, error bars, or methodology — the actual paper may be solid, but cannot be assessed from this source alone.
human_spotchecks: Verify whether the Nature Physics paper (DOI: 10.1038/s41567-025-02972-z) cites Crossno et al. (Science 2016) and explicitly defines what is novel relative to prior observations of the Dirac fluid and WF breakdown in graphene — this determines whether the false-novelty problem is confined to the press release or originates in the paper itself.

Check whether the factor-of-200 Wiedemann-Franz Lorenz-number enhancement is measured across a temperature and gate-voltage range or at a single fine-tuned operating point, and how it compares to Crossno et al.'s ~10× enhancement at comparable conditions (temperature, carrier density, disorder level).

Verify the reported viscosity-to-entropy-density ratio (η/s) and confirm whether it approaches the KSS holographic bound (ℏ/4πk_B) — the press release says 'closest possible' but the Nature Physics abstract reportedly says 'within a factor of four'; expert reading of the supplement is needed to assess which is accurate and how the extraction was performed.

Confirm whether the universality claim (material-independent quantum-of-conductance scaling for both charge and heat conductivity) is demonstrated across multiple device preparations, carrier densities near the Dirac point, and temperature ranges — or only at a single operating condition — since universality by definition requires parameter-independent scaling evidence.
public_summary: The paper bundle is a ScienceDaily press release (September 2025) from the Indian Institute of Science summarizing a Nature Physics publication by Majumdar et al. on charge and heat transport in ultraclean graphene. The press release claims graphene "shattered" the Wiedemann-Franz (WF) law for the first time and that electrons were observed behaving as a nearly perfect quantum fluid, analogous to quark-gluon plasma at CERN. The review board identified two major factual problems with this framing: (1) the WF law is a Sommerfeld-expansion result for Fermi liquids and was theoretically predicted to fail at graphene's charge-neutral Dirac point decades ago, so its absence there is expected physics, not a broken law; (2) the Dirac fluid state and WF breakdown in graphene were already experimentally reported by Crossno et al. (Science 2016) and Bandurin et al. (Science 2016) using similar hBN-encapsulated ultraclean techniques — the "for the first time" framing is false. All three reviewers in both cycles converged on these two objections. The most credible contribution of the underlying paper, per its actual title ("Universality in quantum critical flow of charge and heat in ultraclean graphene"), appears to be the precise demonstration that both charge and heat conductivity scale with a material-independent universal constant at the quantum-critical point, plus a larger WF violation factor (~200×) than previously measured. Claims connecting the result to black-hole thermodynamics, quark-gluon plasma equivalence, and next-generation quantum sensors are speculative extrapolations from theoretical analogies and carry no supporting evidence in the reviewed document. The underlying research may be a sound incremental advance appropriate for Nature Physics, but the press release as a document is substantially oversold.
reviewer_disagreements: Cycle 1 disagreement on novelty severity: Gemini initially rated the 'first time' novelty overstatement as 'minor' severity on the grounds that the universality measurement may be genuinely new; Claude and Codex argued it constitutes a 'major' factual error that materially misleads readers about the state of the field. By cycle 2, Gemini upgraded this to 'major' after Claude cited specific prior-art references.

Cycle 1 disagreement on technical soundness score: Gemini gave the press release a technical_soundness score of 5/10, reflecting inferred quality of the underlying Nature Physics paper; Claude argued 4/10 is more appropriate when scoring the press release as the actual reviewed artifact, given its false framing and complete absence of auditable methodology. This difference persisted into cycle 2 (Gemini: 3, Claude: 4).

Cycle 1 disagreement on novelty score: Codex assigned novelty 5/10, reflecting potential significance of the universality result; all other reviewers scored it 3–4/10, arguing the core Dirac-fluid phenomenon is 2016 prior art. Codex revised down to 3.5/10 in cycle 2 after accepting the prior-art objection.

Minor disagreement on how to characterize the universality claim: Gemini marked it 'well_supported' in cycle 1, inferring from Nature Physics publication that peer review validated it; Claude and Codex held it 'partially_supported' on the grounds that the press release itself provides no formula, scaling variable, or parameter range — the press release as artifact cannot support what the paper proves.

strongest_objections.objection: The press release headline and summary claim graphene 'broke' or 'shattered' a fundamental law of physics, but the Wiedemann-Franz law is a Sommerfeld-expansion result valid only for weakly interacting Fermi liquids. At graphene's Dirac point — a non-Fermi-liquid, quantum-critical state with equal electron-hole populations — theory has predicted WF failure since the mid-2000s (Müller et al., Fritz et al.). Observing the expected breakdown is not breaking a fundamental law; it is confirming a known limitation of a regime-specific approximation.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Title, Summary, and main body paragraphs describing the Wiedemann-Franz law

strongest_objections.objection: The 'for the first time' novelty framing is factually false. Crossno et al. (Science 351, 1058–1061, 2016) already reported experimental observation of the Dirac fluid and breakdown of the Wiedemann-Franz law in hBN-encapsulated graphene using ultraclean sample techniques. Bandurin et al. (Science 2016) independently reported viscous electron flow. The press release does not acknowledge this prior art or clarify what distinguishes the 2025 result.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Summary paragraph and first two body paragraphs

strongest_objections.objection: The reviewed artifact is a press release with no data, figures, error bars, Lorenz-ratio values, temperature ranges, phonon-subtraction methodology, or sample parameters. The factor-of-200 WF violation, the universality claim, and the viscosity result are all unauditable from the submitted document. Strong quantitative claims cannot be assigned any evidentiary weight without the primary paper.

The claim that the Dirac fluid viscosity is 'the closest possible to a perfect fluid' is not supported by any quantitative comparison in the press release. No dimensionless viscosity-to-entropy ratio (η/s), no entropy density, and no reference to the KSS holographic bound (η/s ≥ ℏ/4πk_B) are provided. The Nature Physics abstract reportedly uses the softer phrase 'within a factor of four of a minimally viscous quantum fluid', which is significantly weaker than 'closest possible'.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Body paragraph beginning 'Since this water-like behaviour...'

strongest_objections.objection: The press release implies direct experimental relevance to black-hole thermodynamics, quark-gluon plasma, and next-generation quantum sensors. The black-hole connection is a theoretical analogy via AdS/CFT holographic duality, not an experimental correspondence. The quantum-sensor implication is purely speculative with no mechanism or supporting evidence provided.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Final two body paragraphs before the topics section

strongest_supported_claims: The paper aims to show that both charge and heat transport in ultraclean graphene near the Dirac point are governed by a material-independent universal constant equal to the quantum of conductance — a claim consistent with the actual Nature Physics paper title and supported by all three reviewers as the most credible novel contribution.

The paper claims a Wiedemann-Franz Lorenz-number enhancement exceeding 200× at low temperatures in ultraclean samples, which if confirmed would represent a quantitative advance over the ~10× enhancement reported by Crossno et al. (Science 2016) at comparable conditions.

The Dirac fluid state — where graphene electrons near charge neutrality behave as a strongly interacting collective fluid with ultra-low viscosity — is a well-established theoretical and experimental concept, and the analogy to quark-gluon plasma is a recognized (if approximate) framework within the field.
technical_takeaway: The reviewed artifact is an IISc institutional press release, not the primary article. The plausible novel contribution of the underlying Majumdar et al. Nature Physics paper is the experimental demonstration of material-independent, quantum-of-conductance-governed scaling of both charge and heat transport at graphene's quantum-critical Dirac point, along with a reported Wiedemann-Franz Lorenz-number enhancement exceeding 200× in ultraclean hBN-encapsulated samples — roughly 20× larger than the ~10× violation reported by Crossno et al. (Science 2016). The "perfect fluid" viscosity claim invokes the AdS/CFT-motivated KSS bound (η/s ≥ ℏ/4πk_B) but supplies no dimensionless ratio, entropy density, or measurement protocol. All quantitative claims are unauditable from the press release; the universality and viscosity results require expert reading of the full paper and supplement.
